data.short_description: Mystery genre pack for the Novel Studio app: clue, red-herring, alibi, and secret thread types, plus fair-play review lenses. A novel adopts the pack in its Blueprint; its threads may then use these types, and reviews run these lenses.

lore.title: Thread Types
lore.content:
Additional thread_type values for novels that adopt this pack. Exact spellings; each entry says what the type is and what its payoff owes the reader.

clue — real evidence that points, however obliquely, toward the true solution. Its payoff is being used: the reveal must rest on it, and FAIR PLAY demands it appeared on the page (planted_in) before the reveal scene — the reader must have had the same chance as the detective.

red-herring — evidence or behavior that points away from the truth. Its payoff is its innocent explanation, delivered on the page: a herring that is simply forgotten reads as a cheat, not a twist.

alibi — a suspect's account of where they were and what they did. Its payoff is a verdict: confirmed on the page, or broken on the page. An alibi left dangling leaves its suspect unresolved.

secret — something a character conceals that is not the crime (an affair, a debt, a past). Secrets are what make innocent suspects act guilty. The payoff is exposure — or a deliberate, noted choice to let it stay buried.

lore.title: Review Lenses
lore.content:
Checks the Review workflow runs for novels that adopt this pack. Report per lens, citing scenes and threads by slug; findings are surfaced, never auto-fixed.

ANCHORS: the reveal scene is the scene the solution's clue threads resolve in (resolved_in) — when that is ambiguous, ask the user to name the reveal. The suspects are the characters carrying alibi or secret threads, plus anyone the user names as one — "suspect" is not a role value.

Fair-Play Check — for every clue thread the solution relies on: does its planted_in scene come before the reveal scene in sequence order? Compare sequence only within one novel — a planted_in in an earlier book always precedes the reveal. A clue first appearing in the reveal itself is a finding. The reader must be able to lose fairly.

Red Herring Audit — every red-herring thread must have a resolved_in pointing at the scene where its innocent explanation lands. Open herrings at the finale are findings: each one is a reader still suspecting the wrong person after the last page.

Suspect Coverage — for each character the story treats as a suspect: are motive, means, and opportunity established on the page, and is their elimination (or guilt) shown in a scene rather than happening offstage? A suspect who simply stops being mentioned is a finding.

Reveal Integrity — read the reveal scene's WHAT CHANGES against the clue threads: does the stated solution use only planted clues, or does it lean on knowledge the detective never visibly acquired? Detective-only knowledge is a finding.
